1.description: The increasing role of intangible capital. Panel A plots aggregate intangible capital scaled by aggregate total capital over the period 1974 to 2021. Intangible capital is equal to the replacement cost of intangible capital calculated using the algorithm developed in Peters and Taylor (2017). Total capital is equal to net property, plant, and equipment plus intangible capital. Variables are constructed using annual aggregated firm-level data. Panel B plots total patents granted. The patent data come from the United States Patent and Trademark Office. See Section I for more details regarding the sample and variable construction.

2.description: The empirical distribution of q for low- and high-intangibility firms. This figure examines the effect of adjusting q for intangible capital on its empirical distribution. The unadjusted q is the adjusted market value of the firm scaled by tangible capital (net property, plant, and equipment). The adjusted market value is equal to the market value of equity plus long-term debt plus the short-term portion of long-term debt minus net working capital (current assets minus current liabilities). The adjusted q is the adjusted market value of the firm scaled by tangible and intangible capital (net property, plant, and equipment plus intangible capital). Intangible capital is equal to the replacement cost of intangible capital calculated using the algorithm developed in Peters and Taylor (2017). Panels A and B report the empirical distributions of the adjusted and unadjusted measures of q for low- and high-intangibility firms, respectively. Intangibility is equal to intangible capital scaled by total capital. We use the level of intangibility at time t – 1 to form the intangibility deciles. Low-intangibility firms are in the smallest decile and high-intangibility firms are in the largest decile. See Section I for more details regarding the sample and variable construction.

3.description: Trends in q and aggregate investment rates. This figure plots aggregate average q and aggregate investment rates over the period 1974 to 2021. The variable itot is equal to the sum of capital expenditures, R&D expenses, and 30% of SG&A expenses scaled by lagged total capital (net property, plant, and equipment plus intangible capital). The variable qtot is equal to the adjusted market value of the firm scaled by total capital. The adjusted market value is equal to the market value of equity plus long-term debt plus the short-term portion of long-term debt minus net working capital (current assets minus current liabilities). Variables are constructed using annual aggregated firm-level data from the Compustat database. See Section I for more details regarding the sample and variable construction.

4.description: Changes in q and investment rates by industry. This figure reports percentage changes in q and investment rates by industry. Percentage changes are calculated as the average level over the period 2019 to 2021 relative to the average level over the period 1974 to 1976. Industries are defined using Fama and French (1997) industry classification codes. The variable qtot is equal to the adjusted market value of the firm scaled by total capital (net property, plant, and equipment plus intangible capital). The adjusted market value is equal to the market value of equity plus long-term debt plus the short-term portion of long-term debt minus net working capital (current assets minus current liabilities). The variable itot is equal to the sum of capital expenditures, R&D expenses, and 30% of SG&A expenses scaled by lagged total capital. Variables are constructed using annual aggregated firm-level data from the Compustat database. See Section I for more details regarding the sample and variable construction.

5.description: The empirical distribution of asset utilization for low- and high-intangibility firms. This figure examines the effect of adjusting asset utilization for intangible capital on its empirical distribution. The unadjusted asset utilization is sales scaled by tangible capital (net property, plant, and equipment). The adjusted asset utilization is sales scaled by tangible and intangible capital (net property, plant, and equipment plus intangible capital). Intangible capital is equal to the replacement cost of intangible capital calculated using the algorithm developed in Peters and Taylor (2017). Panels A and B report the empirical distributions of the adjusted and unadjusted measures of asset utilization for low- and high-intangibility firms, respectively. Intangibility is equal to intangible capital scaled by total capital. We use the level of intangibility at time t – 1 to form the intangibility deciles. Low-intangibility firms are in the smallest decile and high-intangibility firms are in the largest decile. See Sections I and III for more details regarding the sample and variable construction.

6.description: Capacity utilization sorted by asset utilization deciles. This figure plots mean capacity utilization sorted by asset utilization deciles, controlling for industry and year fixed effects. Capacity utilization reflects managers’ assessments of the fraction of their total capacity being utilized to produce finished goods. This measure is primarily derived from the U.S. Census Bureau's quarterly surveys and is available at the industry level for the manufacturing, mining, and electric and gas utilities sectors. Asset utilization is defined as sales scaled by total capital (net property, plant, and equipment plus intangible capital). See Sections I and III for more details regarding the sample and variable construction.

7.description: Proxies for financial frictions by total capital deciles. This figure reports several proxies for financial frictions by total capital deciles. Total capital is equal to net property, plant, and equipment plus intangible capital. We use the total capital at time t – 1 to form the deciles. Panel A reports average net cash flows (income before extraordinary items plus depreciation minus capital expenditures scaled by lagged total capital). Panel B reports average net total payouts (dividends plus repurchases less equity issues scaled by lagged total capital). Panels C, D, and E report the fraction of firms with rated debt, investment-grade debt (BBB-or above), and commercial paper, respectively.

8.description: Out-of-sample investment intensity forecasts. This figure plots actual total investment rates against three different out-of-sample forecasts of the same measure over the period 1995 to 2021. The variable itot is equal to the sum of capital expenditures, R&D expenses, and 30% of SG&A expenses scaled by lagged total capital (net property, plant, and equipment plus intangible capital). We run 20-year recursive regressions to forecast itot at time t using only the information available at time t – 1 for both the traditional q model and the augmented model. The training period is from 1974 to 1994. Forecasts from the naïve model are defined as the average investment rate over the past 20 years. Variables are constructed using annual aggregated firm-level data. Panel A (B) reports results from rolling-(expanding-) window recursive regressions. See Sections I and III for more details regarding the sample and variable construction.

9.description: Trends in asset utilization and capacity utilization. Panel A plots the trend in aggregate asset utilization for sample firms over the period 1974 to 2021. Asset utilization is equal to sales scaled by total capital (net property, plant, and equipment plus intangible capital). This variable is constructed using annual aggregated firm-level data. Panel B plots the U.S. Capacity Utilization, Total Index, seasonally adjusted on a monthly basis from January 1967 through May 2021. This data set is maintained by the Federal Reserve and is available at https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/TCU. Gray shading indicates NBER-designated recessions. The index summarizes survey information gathered from plant managers in the manufacturing, mining, and electric and gas utilities sectors at facilities located in the United States.

10.description: Changes in asset utilization by industry. This figure reports percentage changes in asset utilization by industry. Percentage changes are calculated as the average level over the period 2019 to 2021 relative to the average level over the period 1974 to 1976. Industries are defined using Fama and French (1997) industry classification codes. Asset utilization is equal to sales scaled by total capital (net property, plant, and equipment plus intangible capital). Variables are constructed using annual aggregated firm-level data. See Section I for more details regarding the sample and variable construction.

11.description: Government data. This figure reports measures of aggregate asset utilization and productive capital calculated using data from Table S.5.a of the Integrated Macroeconomic Accounts as reported by the Bureau of Economic Analysis. Panel A plots the ratio of gross value-added (Line 1) to nonfinancial assets (Line 109). Panel B plots tangible capital scaled by total capital as well as intangible capital scaled by total capital. Tangible capital is equal to real estate (Line 110) plus equipment (Line 111) plus inventory (Line 113). Intangible capital is equal to intellectual property products (Line 112). Panel C plots all the components of total capital relative to total capital.

12.description: Government data: Counterfactual exercise. This figure reports the results from two counterfactual exercises designed to assess the impact of the BEA's underestimation of intangibles on asset utilization. In the first exercise (Counterfactual 1), we modify the level of intangibles reported by the BEA by adding to it Peters and Taylor's (2017) estimates of organizational capital and intangibles revealed during M&A transactions. In the second exercise (Counterfactual 2), we alter the original level of intangible assets reported by the BEA such that the intangible-to-physical capital ratio using their data matches that observed using Peters and Taylor's (2017) more comprehensive method. We then take these two hypothetical time series of intangible assets and calculate two new hypothetical time series of asset utilization. For comparison purposes, we scale each series by its respective level in 1974.

13.description: Asset utilization during recessionary and expansionary periods. Panel A plots the average proportional change in asset utilization obtained from Compustat, conditional on whether a given year was classified as expansionary or recessionary according to the NBER definition. Asset utilization is equal to sales scaled by total capital (gross property, plant, and equipment plus intangible capital). Panel B plots (i) the cumulative mean CPI, median CPI, mean PPI, and median PPI over the same time period (left y-axis), as well as (ii) the mean monthly capacity utilization rate around the six recessions between 1970 and 2020, normalized respective to its level at the trough in each recession (right y-axis).

14.description: Changes in q, investment rates, and asset utilization by country. This figure reports percentage changes in average q, investment rates, and asset utilization by country. Percentage changes are calculated as the average level over the period 2019 to 2021 relative to the average level over the period 1981 to 1983. The variable qtot is equal to the adjusted market value of the firm scaled by total capital (net property, plant, and equipment plus intangible capital). The adjusted market value is equal to the market value of equity plus total assets minus book value of equity minus net working capital (current assets minus current liabilities). The variable itot is equal to the sum of capital expenditures, R&D expenses, and 30% of SG&A expenses scaled by lagged total capital. The variable iassetu is equal to sales scaled by total capital. Variables are constructed using annual aggregated firm-level data from the Worldscope database. See Section VII for more details regarding the sample and variable construction.
